question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Massachusetts, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Massachusetts Public Records Act, state agencies, including those administering Medicaid (MassHealth), must respond to a public records request within 10 business days of receiving it. Within this timeframe, the agency's Records Access Officer must either grant access to the records, deny the request with a detailed explanation of the specific legal exemption being invoked (such as medical privacy laws), or provide written notice that the response will be delayed and provide an estimated date for when the records will be available.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Massachusetts, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?

answer_extracted: Under the Texas Public Information Act (TPIA), all state agencies, including the Texas Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC) which administers Medicaid, generally have 10 business days from the date a request is received to respond. In their response, the agency must either provide the public information, explain why the information is exempt from disclosure (for instance, specific patient medical data is typically protected under HIPAA and TPIA privacy exemptions), or ask the requestor to narrow a voluminous request. Note: If you are referring to the processing time for a Medicaid benefits application rather than a public record request, the state generally has 45 days to process a standard application.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Texas, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Texas Public Information Act (TPIA), state agencies—including the Texas Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC), which administers the Medicaid program—generally have 10 business days to respond to a public records request. Within this 10-business-day window, the agency must either release the requested information, certify in writing that the information does not exist, or request an open records ruling from the Texas Attorney General if they intend to withhold any part of the response. Note that individual Medicaid beneficiary case files and medical records are generally exempt from public disclosure under the TPIA due to federal HIPAA and state privacy laws, though beneficiaries can request their own records through separate medical records release processes.
source: Texas Attorney General's Office - Open Government Division (The Public Information Act, Texas Government Code Chapter 552) and Texas Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC) Public Information Request guidelines.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In New York, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under New York State's Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), specifically Public Officers Law Article 6, state and local agencies (including the Department of Health and local district social services departments that administer Medicaid) have five (5) business days from the receipt of a written request to respond. Within this five-business-day window, the agency must either: (1) make the records available, (2) deny the request in writing while citing the specific legal exemption for the denial—such as protections for personal health information under federal HIPAA or New York Public Health Law § 18—or (3) provide a written acknowledgment of the request along with an estimated date for when a final determination will be made.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In New York, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted:
Under New York State's Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), state and local agencies (including the Department of Health and local departments of social services that administer Medicaid) have five (5) business days to respond to a public records request. Within this timeframe, the agency must either grant access to the requested records, deny the request in writing stating the reason for denial (for example, specific patient Medicaid records are generally exempt from public disclosure under Public Health Law § 2913 due to confidentiality), or provide a written acknowledgment of receipt along with an approximate date by which the request will be granted or denied. 

(Note: If your question refers to the processing time for a Medicaid eligibility application for benefits rather than a public records request, local agencies generally have 45 days to make a determination, or up to 90 days if a disability determination is required.)
source: New York State Department of State, Committee on Open Government - A Guide to the Freedom of Information Law (Public Officers Law Article 6, § 87)

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Texas, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Texas Public Information Act (TPIA), state agencies such as the Texas Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC), which administers Medicaid, generally have 10 business days to respond to a public records request. Within this 10-business-day window, the agency must either: (1) provide the requested Medicaid records; (2) notify the requester of the cost and a realistic timeline for when the records will be available for pickup or mailing; or (3) request an open records ruling from the Texas Attorney General if the agency intends to withhold any part of the records as confidential (such as private medical or personal information protected by HIPAA or the Texas Medical Records Privacy Act).
source: Texas Government Code, Chapter 552 (Texas Public Information Act) and the Texas Attorney General's Public Information Handbook

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
prompt_sent: In Massachusetts, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on Medicaid?
answer_extracted: Under the Massachusetts Public Records Law (M.G.L. c. 66, § 10), state agencies, including those administering Medicaid (MassHealth), generally have 10 business days from receipt of a request to respond. However, while the agency is required to issue a formal response within this 10-business-day window, individual Medicaid records containing protected health information (PHI) or personally identifiable information (PII) are exempt from public disclosure under federal privacy laws (HIPAA) and Massachusetts state law (M.G.L. c. 111, § 70E). Therefore, an agency's response to a request for specific patient Medicaid records is typically a formal denial or the release of heavily redacted, non-identifying aggregate data.

{
  "answer": "Under the Texas Public Information Act (TPIA), state agencies such as the Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC), which administers Medicaid, generally have 10 business days from the receipt of a written request to respond. Within this 10-business-day window, the agency must either release the requested records, certify a specific date and time when the records will be available for inspection, or request an open records ruling from the Texas Attorney General's Office if they intend to withhold any of the information as legally exempt (such as protected patient health information).",
  "source": "Texas Public Information Act (Chapter 552 of the Texas Government Code) and Texas Attorney General Open Government Division: https://www.texasattorneygeneral.gov/open-government",
  "confidence": "high"
}

answer_extracted: Under the Texas Public Information Act (TPIA), state agencies such as the Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC), which administers Medicaid, generally have 10 business days from the receipt of a written request to respond. Within this 10-business-day window, the agency must either release the requested records, certify a specific date and time when the records will be available for inspection, or request an open records ruling from the Texas Attorney General's Office if they intend to withhold any of the information as legally exempt (such as protected patient health information).
source: Texas Public Information Act (Chapter 552 of the Texas Government Code) and Texas Attorney General Open Government Division: https://www.texasattorneygeneral.gov/open-government
